formations_blocks: South Korea three-man backline in possession
South Korea pressing high/pinning South Africa in their half
South Africa dealing with the Korean press and defending deeper
key_matchups: Kim Min-jae vs Aubrey Modiba on the near-post corner
Kim Min-jae contesting multiple South African aerial/shot situations
Kim Seung-gyu vs South Africa's high-cross deliveries
Makgopa vs the inattentive Korean defense on the aerial ball
phases.summary: South Korea’s setup is immediately clear: a three-man backline staying high enough to sustain pressure, with South Africa forced to play through an aggressive Korean press and spend spells pinned back. The opening 15 minutes are frantic and chance-heavy, driven by set pieces and direct balls, with Kim Min-jae repeatedly decisive at both ends and South Africa also threatening via crosses and second balls.

formations_blocks: South Korea attacking with crosses and set-piece pressure
South Africa in a reactive shape, then quickly countering into open space
South Africa’s pressing around the South Korean defensive third
adjustments: South Korea reset after being exposed by turnovers
South Korea switch from left-side attacks to work down the right by 26'
Both sides lean heavily on set pieces and direct balls after open-play attacks stall
key_matchups: Lee Gi-hyuk vs Maseko: recovery block on a 3-v-2 break
Mbatha vs the South Korean back line: threaded pass and shot chance at 30'
Kim Seung-gyu vs South Africa’s attackers: comfortable save, then double-save sequence
Mbatha vs Yang Hyun-jun: midfield foul after South Africa swarm the box
phases.summary: This phase settles into a tense, transitional contest: South Korea have more sustained possession and several corner/free-kick situations, but South Africa repeatedly threaten by forcing turnovers and breaking quickly into space. Neither side establishes complete control, and the game increasingly becomes about second balls, direct passes, and set-piece danger rather than clean buildup play.

adjustments: South Korea made a triple substitution at halftime: Son Heung-min, Kim Jin-gyu and Jens Castrop came on for Paik Seung-ho, Lee Tae-seok and Hwang Hee-chan
South Korea appear to be reacting to a poor final 30 minutes and a missed opportunity at the end of the first half
key_matchups: South Africa vs South Korea attacking flurries around the South African goal, including a double save and a likely offside chance for Makgopa
phases.summary: The halftime picture is one of South Korea underperforming relative to expectations, with the commentary stressing that they were fortunate to reach the break level after looking less sharp for the last half hour. South Africa created the clearest late opening, forcing a double save in a flurry that nearly led to a goal for Makgopa, while South Korea responded with a substantial halftime personnel change to reset the match.

formations_blocks: South Africa scale back the pressure
South Korea in possession for long spells after the restart
adjustments: South Africa dropped off slightly after halftime to allow South Korea more of the ball
Maseko became the lone South African attacking threat
key_matchups: Mudau vs a South Korean attack on the edge of South Africa's area
Maseko vs two South Korea defenders
Son vs South Africa from distance and through central runs
Castrop as the left-sided outlet for South Korea
phases.summary: The second half opened with South Korea holding sustained possession, but they continued to struggle to turn territory into clear chances. South Africa initially eased their pressure, then relied on defensive interventions and Maseko as the main outlet in transition, with his movement repeatedly causing problems before he scored the decisive goal in the 63rd minute.

formations_blocks: South Africa defending in a shape set up to contest runs over the top
South Africa counter-attacking/blocking centrally while looking to spring forward
adjustments: South Africa substitution: Mofokeng off, Jaylen Adams on
South Korea increasingly using direct balls over the top and crosses from wide areas
South Korea begin to show more creativity late, especially down the left
key_matchups: Cho vs South African back line
Lee Kang-in vs South African defense
Son vs multiple defenders in the box
phases.summary: This phase is defined by South Korea’s high line and South Africa’s willingness to attack that space with direct, parallel runs and counters, though neither side really turns that into clear chances. South Korea spend long spells circulating possession without imagination, then become more dangerous late through wide deliveries, set pieces, and a brief uptick in creativity. The most notable individual duels are South African defenders repeatedly confronting Son and South Korea’s creators trying to thread balls through a crowded defensive block.
